title: Functional Information: Measuring Complexity That Matters
subtitle: From combination locks to neural networks — why the universe keeps getting more interesting

props.title: 🔒 ELI5 — Why Do Some Things Work Better Than Others?

children.children: Imagine you find a combination lock with 6 digits. Each digit can be 0–9, so there are a million possible combinations. But only ONE opens the lock.

children: Think of functional information as a measure of how SPECIAL a working thing is. If you have a 6-digit combination lock, there are 1,000,000 possible codes but only 1 opens it. That's really special — and functional information tells you exactly HOW special: about 20 bits

children.children: Now imagine a sloppy lock where ANY code starting with '3' opens it. That's 100,000 out of 1,000,000 — one in ten. Not very special at all. Functional information: only about 3.3 bits.
The harder it is to stumble onto a working combination by chance, the more functional information it has.
⚠️ "about 20 bits":

children: AI generation failed: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable not set. Please try again.

children.children: Lock A: Only 1 in 1,000,000 combinations works.
20 bits
Lock B: 1 in 10 combinations works.

children: FI = −log₂(1/10) ≈

children.children: 3.3 bits
More bits = rarer = more special = higher functional information.
Functional information doesn't care WHICH combination opens the lock — just how hard it is to find ANY working one by chance. It measures the rarity of function, not the identity of the solution.

props.title: 🌍 ELI12 — The Universe Gets More Interesting Over Time

children.children: In 2023, scientists Robert Hazen and colleagues published a landmark paper in PNAS proposing a new law of nature: systems that have the right ingredients tend to get more complex over time. They called it the Law of Increasing Functional Information.
The recipe is simple: take diverse components, add selection (some configurations survive and others don't), wait. Complexity increases.
Minerals:

children: When Earth formed 4.5 billion years ago, there were only about 250 mineral species. Today there are over 6,000. Each new mineral represents a rarer, more specific arrangement of atoms — higher functional information.

children.children: Biology:

children: Early life was single-celled. Over billions of years, selection produced multicellular organisms, nervous systems, brains. The fraction of possible molecular arrangements that produce a working brain is incomprehensibly small — astronomical FI.

children.children: Language:

children: Early human communication was simple signals. Today we have grammar, metaphor, poetry, legal contracts. The functional information of language has been increasing for millennia.

Common misconception: FI is NOT the same as entropy! Entropy measures disorder — how spread out energy is. Functional information measures how rare a WORKING configuration is. A pile of sand has high entropy but low functional information
. A watch has lower entropy but high functional information — very few arrangements of gears actually tell time.

children.children: ⚠️ "A pile of sand has high entropy but low functional information":

children: AI generation failed: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable not set. Please try again.

children.children: ⚠️ "A pile of sand has high entropy but low functional information":

children: AI generation failed: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable not set. Please try again.

children.children: Imagine a library where books appear randomly on shelves. Most are gibberish. But there's a rule: books that get read survive; unread books vanish. Over time, the library fills with increasingly interesting books. The 'functional information' of the library keeps rising — the books on the shelves become rarer and rarer members of the set of all possible letter sequences.
That's Wong & Hazen's law in action: diverse components (random letter sequences) + selection (readers keep the good ones) + time = increasing functional information.

props.title: 📐 High School — Measuring Complexity That Matters

children.children: Now let's get precise. Functional information has a formula, and it's beautifully simple.
Here's what each piece means:
I(Ex)

children: = the functional information at function threshold Ex (measured in bits)

children.children: F(Ex)

children: = the fraction of ALL possible configurations that achieve at least function level Ex

children.children: −log₂

children: = the negative log base 2, which converts a fraction into bits

children.children: That's it. Count the fraction of configurations that work, take the negative log. The rarer the working configurations, the more bits of functional information.
Why log₂?

children: Because it gives us bits — the same unit as computer memory. If F(Ex) = 1/2, that's 1 bit. If F(Ex) = 1/1024, that's 10 bits. Each bit doubles the rarity.

children.children: Consider short RNA sequences of length 40. Each position can be A, C, G, or U, so there are 4⁴⁰ ≈ 1.2 × 10²⁴ possible sequences.
Function: fold into ANY stable structure

children: — Maybe 1 in 10 sequences fold stably. F(Ex) = 0.1, so I = −log₂(0.1) ≈ 3.3 bits.

children.children: Function: catalyze a specific reaction

children: — Perhaps 1 in 10¹⁵ sequences do this. F(Ex) = 10⁻¹⁵, so I = −log₂(10⁻¹⁵) ≈ 50 bits.

children.children: Same configuration space, but a harder function requirement yields much higher FI. The threshold Ex matters enormously.
How does this compare to other ways of measuring information?
Shannon information

children: asks: how surprising is this MESSAGE? (Focus: communication)

children.children: Kolmogorov complexity

children: asks: what's the shortest DESCRIPTION of this object? (Focus: structure)

children.children: Functional information

children: asks: how rare is something that DOES this? (Focus: function)

children.children: Common misconception: FI ≠ Kolmogorov complexity! A random string has HIGH Kolmogorov complexity (hard to describe) but ZERO functional information (it doesn't DO anything). FI cares about what a thing accomplishes, not how complex its description is.
Let's formalize what we've been building intuitively.

children: Given a system with configuration space

children.children: Ω

children: (the set of all possible states), and a function

children.children: f: Ω → ℝ

children: that measures how well each configuration performs some task, the functional information at threshold Ex is:

children.children: I(Ex) = −log₂( |{ω ∈ Ω : f(ω) ≥ Ex}| / |Ω| )
The numerator counts configurations meeting the threshold. The denominator is the total number of configurations. Their ratio is F(Ex).
Key insight: FI depends on the threshold.

children: As you raise Ex (demand better performance), fewer configurations qualify, F(Ex) shrinks, and FI increases. This gives you a whole CURVE of functional information, not just a single number.

props.title: The Information Triangle: Shannon vs. Kolmogorov vs. FI vs. MDL

children.children: These four measures form a conceptual map of 'information':
Shannon Information H(X)

children: — Expected surprise of a random variable. Asks: 'How much do I learn by observing this?' Defined over probability distributions. Units: bits. Domain: communication theory.

children.children: Kolmogorov Complexity K(x)

children: — Length of the shortest program that outputs x. Asks: 'How complex IS this object?' Uncomputable in general. Units: bits. Domain: algorithmic information theory.

children.children: Functional Information I(Ex)

children: — Negative log of the fraction of configurations achieving function level Ex. Asks: 'How hard is it to find something that WORKS this well?' Depends on function threshold. Units: bits. Domain: evolving systems, search.

children.children: Minimum Description Length (MDL)

children: — Best model minimizes description length + data given model. Asks: 'What's the right level of complexity for the model?' Practical approximation of Kolmogorov. Units: bits. Domain: model selection, statistical learning.

children.children: The key relationships: Kolmogorov asks 'how complex IS it?', FI asks 'how complex does it NEED to be?', MDL asks 'how complex SHOULD the model be?', Shannon asks 'how much did I LEARN?'
Connection to search:

children: FI measures the difficulty of a search problem. If you're randomly sampling configurations from Ω, the expected number of samples before finding one with f(ω) ≥ Ex is 1/F(Ex) = 2^(I(Ex)). High FI = exponentially hard search.

children.children: Assembly theory connection:

children: Assembly theory (Cronin & Walker) measures molecular complexity by counting the minimum assembly steps to build a molecule from basic parts. This is related but distinct: assembly index measures construction difficulty (like Kolmogorov), while FI measures functional rarity. A molecule can be simple to assemble but functionally rare, or complex to assemble but functionally common.

children.children:
import numpy as np

def functional_information(config_space_size, num_functional):
    """Compute FI in bits.
    
    Args:
        config_space_size: total number of possible configurations |Ω|
        num_functional: configurations meeting threshold |{ω: f(ω) ≥ Ex}|
    Returns:
        FI in bits
    """
    F_ex = num_functional / config_space_size
    return -np.log2(F_ex)

# Example 1: Combination lock (1 in 1,000,000)
print(f"6-digit lock: {functional_information(1_000_000, 1):.1f} bits")
# → 20.0 bits

# Example 2: RNA folding (1 in 10)
print(f"RNA fold:     {functional_information(10, 1):.1f} bits")
# → 3.3 bits

# Example 3: RNA catalysis (1 in 10^15)
print(f"RNA catalyze: {functional_information(10**15, 1):.1f} bits")
# → 49.8 bits

# Example 4: FI curve — vary threshold
print("\nFI vs. threshold for a population of 10,000 configs:")
for fraction in [0.5, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001]:
    fi = -np.log2(fraction)
    print(f"  F(Ex) = {fraction:.4f} → FI = {fi:.1f} bits")
Here's where it gets exciting. We can apply functional information theory directly to neural networks — and it reframes everything we know about training and mechanistic interpretability.
The key mapping: Gradient descent IS selection. Weight space IS the configuration space. Loss function IS the fitness function. Training IS evolution.

Wong & Hazen's law says: systems with diverse components, under selection, increase in functional information over time. A neural network during training has diverse components (millions of weight parameters), strong selection (gradient descent minimizing loss), and time (training steps). The law predicts that networks should converge on increasingly rare, high-FI weight configurations — which is exactly what we observe.

props.title: FI in Neural Networks: Bloom Filter Heads as Case Study

children.children: The question:

children: Consider a single attention head with a 64×64 weight matrix. That's 4,096 parameters. If each can take, say, 1,000 distinct values (after quantization), the configuration space has ~10^(12,000) possible weight matrices. What fraction of those implement membership testing — the Bloom filter function?

children.children: The answer is: an incomprehensibly tiny fraction. Bloom filter heads (like L0H1 and L0H5 in GPT-2) have extremely high functional information. And yet, multiple independently trained models (GPT-2, Pythia, others) converge on these same solutions.
This IS Wong & Hazen's law in action.

children: Independent systems under similar selection pressures converge on the same high-FI solutions. It's convergent evolution — just like how eyes evolved independently in vertebrates and mollusks.

children.children: Contrast with a head like L3H0 (which just attends to all prefix content). Many weight configurations achieve this function — low FI. The genuine Bloom heads require very specific weight configurations for near-zero miss rate AND low false-positive rate — high FI.
Schankian operators and FI:

children: Different semantic operators require different amounts of functional information. Simple operators like ATRANS (physical transfer: 'give', 'take') correspond to common weight patterns — relatively low FI. Complex operators like MBUILD (constructing new mental content: 'decide', 'realize') require rarer configurations — high FI. This predicts that high-FI operators should appear later in training, require more parameters, and be harder to find via mechanistic interpretability.

children.children: Dynamic epistemic heads:

children: FI scales with belief-nesting depth. Tracking 'John believes X' (level 1) requires moderate FI. Tracking 'John believes Mary knows Sue said X' (level 3) requires exponentially more FI — the fraction of weight configurations that correctly process 3-deep belief nesting is vastly smaller. This formalizes the empirical observation that transformers struggle with deep theory-of-mind reasoning.

children.children: The Carver Problem as FI overflow:

children: When a task's required FI exceeds the network's capacity (bounded by architecture and parameter count), the system can't maintain a functional solution. It collapses to the lowest-FI output that minimally satisfies the loss function — which in practice means generic, vague, platitudinous text. The Carver Problem (where models produce bland summaries of complex fiction) is literally functional information overflow.

children.children: Testable predictions from the FI framework:
1. Operators with higher FI appear later in training
2. Higher-FI operators require more parameters
3. Cross-model convergence is strongest for high-FI, high-utility functions
4. Operator FI correlates inversely with natural language frequency
5. Epistemic nesting depth at which performance collapses can be predicted from architectural FI capacity

props.title: 🌌 The Big Picture — Why This Matters

children: The deepest claim of functional information theory is that

children.children: the same law governs mineral evolution, biological evolution, and neural network training

children: . In each case, you have a vast configuration space, a selection mechanism, and time — and the result is convergence on increasingly rare, high-FI solutions.

children.children: This is remarkable. It suggests that complexity isn't an accident — it's an inevitable consequence of selection operating on combinatorial systems. The law of increasing functional information might be as fundamental as the second law of thermodynamics, but pointing in the opposite direction: where entropy describes the tendency toward disorder, FI describes the tendency toward functional complexity under selection.
FI bridges disciplines:
• Geologists can measure the FI of mineral assemblages to track planetary evolution
• Biologists can measure the FI of proteins to quantify how 'designed' they appear
• AI researchers can measure the FI of learned circuits to understand what gradient descent discovers
• All three are instances of the same underlying process
Open questions:
• Can we precisely measure the FI capacity of a neural architecture (not just the weight count, but how much 'functional rarity' it can encode)?
• Is there a phase transition — a critical FI threshold beyond which gradient descent fails to find solutions?
• Can FI predict which capabilities will emerge at which model scale?
• Does the rate of FI increase follow a universal scaling law across domains?

questions.question: A combination lock has 10,000 possible codes and 1 correct code. What is the functional information?
Which of these has the HIGHEST functional information?
questions.explanation: A working watch requires an extremely specific arrangement of components — the fraction of possible gear arrangements that actually tell time is tiny. The rock, random string, and shuffled deck don't require specific configurations to 'be' what they are.

questions.question: What's the key difference between Kolmogorov complexity and functional information?
options: Kolmogorov is measured in bits, FI is not
Kolmogorov measures description length, FI measures functional rarity
FI is always larger than Kolmogorov complexity
They are the same thing with different names
questions.explanation: Kolmogorov complexity asks 'how long is the shortest description?' (structure-focused). FI asks 'how rare is something that does this?' (function-focused). A random string has high Kolmogorov complexity but zero FI.

questions.question: According to Wong & Hazen, what three ingredients are needed for increasing functional information?
questions.explanation: The Law of Increasing Functional Information requires: (1) diverse components that can combine in many ways, (2) selection that preserves functional configurations, and (3) time for the process to operate.

questions.question: In neural networks, gradient descent plays the role of _____ in FI theory.
questions.explanation: Gradient descent IS selection — it preferentially moves toward weight configurations that minimize loss (achieve higher function). Weight space is the configuration space, and loss is the function measure.

questions.question: Why do Bloom filter heads in GPT-2 represent high functional information?
options: They were trained on a lot of data
Only a tiny fraction of possible weight matrices implement membership testing
They appear in the first layer
questions.explanation: FI measures what fraction of configurations achieve a function. The vast majority of possible 64×64 weight matrices do NOT implement Bloom-filter-like membership testing — so the ones that do represent extremely rare, high-FI solutions.

questions.question: If raising the function threshold Ex causes FI to increase, what does this mean practically?
options: Better performance is easier to achieve
Fewer configurations meet the higher bar — working solutions become rarer
questions.explanation: Higher threshold → fewer qualifying configurations → smaller F(Ex) → larger −log₂(F(Ex)) → higher FI. Demanding better performance makes working solutions exponentially rarer.
